title: Assignment 1
text:
1 Welcome
This site contains my Quarto project for Assignment 1.
You’ll find:

Day 1 Practical Answers
Day 3 (questions 3 &4 ) Practical Answers
Day 4 Practical Answers

Use the navigation bar above to explore each section.

title: 2  Day 1 Practical
text:
2.1 Question 1
Find all rows in “airquality” that have missing values. Note that the airquality dataset in R is always available

title: 2  Day 1 Practical
text:
2.1.1 Answer:

#loaded in dataset for airquality, it is already available in r
data <- airquality
head(data)

  Ozone Solar.R Wind Temp Month Day
1    41     190  7.4   67     5   1
2    36     118  8.0   72     5   2
3    12     149 12.6   74     5   3
4    18     313 11.5   62     5   4
5    NA      NA 14.3   56     5   5
6    28      NA 14.9   66     5   6



#the function runs through all the rows and columns to find the rows which contains a NA vaue and appends the row number to the matrix null_rows

null_rows <- data[rowSums(is.na(data))>0,] 
as.integer(rownames(null_rows)) # rownames outputs a string value for the row number but I wanted it as a integer typ so i converted it

 [1]   5   6  10  11  25  26  27  32  33  34  35  36  37  39  42  43  45  46  52
[20]  53  54  55  56  57  58  59  60  61  65  72  75  83  84  96  97  98 102 103
[39] 107 115 119 150

title: 2  Day 1 Practical
text:
2.2 Question 2
Find mean, sd, min, max for each of temperature and ozone level.

2.2.1 Answer:
There are NA values in our dataset, so they need to be accounted for before we can find some summary statistics for Ozone and Temperature in our data.

# For Ozone

cat(" Ozone statistics: \n mean = ", round(mean(data$Ozone, na.rm = TRUE),2), "\n",
"standard deviation = ", round(sd(data$Ozone, na.rm = TRUE),2), "\n", 
"min = " , min(data$Wind, na.rm = TRUE), "\n",
"max = ", max(data$Ozone, na.rm = TRUE),"\n")

 Ozone statistics: 
 mean =  42.13 
 standard deviation =  32.99 
 min =  1.7 
 max =  168 

# For Temperature

cat("\n Temperature statistics: \n mean = " , round(mean(data$Temp, na.rm = TRUE),2), "\n",
"standrard deviation =" ,  round(sd(data$Temp, na.rm = TRUE),2), "\n",
"min = ", min(data$Temp, na.rm = TRUE), "\n",
"max = ", max(data$Temp, na.rm = TRUE))


 Temperature statistics: 
 mean =  77.88 
 standrard deviation = 9.47 
 min =  56 
 max =  97

title: 2  Day 1 Practical
text:
2.3 Question 3
Fit a simple linear regression model to these data, i.e. find the \(beta\) estimates, using the equation above, and matrix calculations in R

2.3.1 Answer:

#loading of dataset
car_data <- cars


# assignment of Y and design matrix(X)
X <- cbind(1,car_data$dist) # made the design matrix X and added a column of 1's for the beta estimate for the constant in the linear regression equation
Y <- car_data$speed


# manually sovling for bhat vector
b_estimate <- solve(t(X)%*%X)%*%t(X)%*%Y
round(b_estimate, 4)

       [,1]
[1,] 8.2839
[2,] 0.1656

title: 2  Day 1 Practical
text:
2.4 Question 4
Check that you get the same estimates as when fitting the linear regression model using lm() in R.

2.4.1 Answer:

model <- lm(Y~X, data = car_data)
round(model$coefficients,4)

(Intercept)          X1          X2 
     8.2839          NA      0.1656 


The beta estimates from the \(lm()\) are the same as the ones from the manual calculation in question 3.

title: 3  Day 3 Practical
text:
3.1 Practical 3
These are my answers for the last 2 questions

title: 3  Day 3 Practical
text:
3.2 Question 4
Plot the function \(f(x) = \sin(x)\) for \(x \in [-2, 2]\).

3.2.1 Answer:

x <- seq(-2,2, 0.01)
sin_fx <- sin(x)
plot(x, sin_fx, "l",
     main ="plot of sin_f(x) function")

title: 3  Day 3 Practical
text:
3.3 Question 5
Generate 1000 values from a \(t\)-distribution with 1 degree of freedom.
Construct a QQ-plot with 95% probability bands (envelopes) to check whether the above values come from a standard normal distribution. You can only use rnorm(), but not qnorm, dnorm, pnorm, or R’s qqplot functions.

3.3.1 Answer:

set.seed(142) # to ensure the t values are reproducable
# sorted both the t values and normal values in order
t_values <- sort(rt( n = 1000, df = 1))
norm_values <- sort(rnorm(1000))

# plotted the t values and a linear line 
plot(norm_values, t_values,
     xlab = "Standard Normal Quantiles",
     ylab = "Sample Quantiles (t df=1)",
     main = "QQ-plot of t-distributed values with a 95% confidence band",
     ylim = c(-5, 5))
abline(0, 1, col = "red") 

# simulated 1000 samples of normal values with 1000 in each sample
sim_num <- 1000
sim_norm <- replicate(sim_num, sort(rnorm(1000)))
# Calculated confidence bands at 95% significance based on normal values 
lower <- apply(sim_norm, 1, quantile, probs = 0.025)
upper <- apply(sim_norm, 1, quantile, probs = 0.975)
# Plottes the lines above
lines(norm_values, lower, col = "blue", lty = 2)
lines(norm_values, upper, col = "blue", lty = 2)








The t-values are not within the confidence and therefore we can conclude that they are not normally distributed.

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.1 Question 1
Display the flights dataset in an alternative format to simply printing it (i.e. running flights).

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.2 Question 2
Rewrite the code using dplyr and the pipe:

4.2.1 Answer:

carrier_dist_stats <- flights %>% 
  filter(month == 1) %>% #filter for the first month
  group_by(carrier) %>% # ensures output shows by carrier the mean and sd distance in average
  summarise(
    mean_distance = mean(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_distance   = sd(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    
  ) %>% 
  arrange(mean_distance, sd_distance)

carrier_dist_stats

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.3 Question 3
Explain why the standard deviation is NA for one carrier, and why it is 0 for others. Demonstrate your answer using code.

4.3.1 Answer:

data <- flights # loaded to variable called data


# looks at the mean of the yv carrier and compares the number or rows in the vector with the number of rows with duplicated values for distance
yv_dist_vec <- flights %>% 
  filter(month ==1) %>% 
  filter(carrier == "YV") %>% 
  select(distance)
rownum <- as.numeric(length(t(yv_dist_vec)))
rownum

[1] 46

duplicated_rows <- sum(duplicated(yv_dist_vec)) 
duplicated_rows+1 # added 1 because it compares with the first row, discounting it as a duplicate

[1] 46


all the distance values for the carrier YV are equal to the mean distance for that carrier. Therefore there is no variance and therefore the standard deviation is 0.

#looks at the number of records of distance for OO carrier
oo_dist_vec <- flights %>% 
  filter(month ==1) %>% 
  filter(carrier == "OO") %>% 
  select(distance)
rownum <- as.numeric(length(t(oo_dist_vec)))
rownum

[1] 1


There is only 1 recorded distance value for the carrier OO, therefore there variance and standard deviation are not applicable

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.4 Question 4
Using tidyr and dplyr where appropriate, construct a dataframe where the carriers are along the columns, and the rows are the average departure delay (dep_delay) flown by each carrier (carrier) in each month.

4.4.1 Answer:

# creates a tibble with average depature delay for each carrier
avg_delay_table <- flights %>% 
  group_by(carrier, month) %>% 
  summarise(avg_dep_delay = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = carrier,
    values_from = avg_dep_delay
  )

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by carrier and month.
ℹ Output is grouped by carrier.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(carrier, month))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.5 Question 5
Calculate the proportion of flights that were delayed (dep_delay greater than 0) but arrived on or before time (arr_delay less than or equal to 0).

4.5.1 Answer:

#creates a tibble with flights satisfying the condition
delay_table <- flights %>% 
  filter(dep_delay > 0 & arr_delay <=0)
# calculates the proportion as a percentage
delayed_num <-nrow(delay_table)
delayed_prop <- (delayed_num / nrow(flights)) *100

round(delayed_prop)

[1] 11


11% of flights were delayed but arrived on/before time.

title: 4  Practical Day 4
text:
4.6 Question 6
Using the airlines and flights datasets, do the following, showing the output from each step:
Identify routes that more than one airline flies

4.6.1 Answer:

#merged the two tables 
airline_flight <- left_join(flights,airlines, by = "carrier")

route <- airline_flight %>% 
  group_by(origin, dest) %>% 
  summarise(carriers_num = n_distinct(carrier)) %>% 
  filter(carriers_num > 1) # filters to show only routes that have more than 1 carrier

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by origin and dest.
ℹ Output is grouped by origin.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(origin, dest))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

route

For each such route, identify the airline with the worst and best average arrival delay.


4.6.2 Answer:

avg_arr_delay <- airline_flight %>% 
  group_by(origin, dest, carrier, name) %>% 
  summarise(mean_delay = mean(arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE))

4.6.3 Answer:

  group_by(origin,dest,name) %>%

Identify the route with the greatest difference between the best and worst performing airlines


4.6.4 Answer:

greatest_diff <- best_worst %>% 
  group_by(origin,dest) %>% 
  mutate(difference = worst_arr - best_arr) %>% 
  arrange(desc(difference))
head(greatest_diff, n = 1)

# A tibble: 1 × 7
# Groups:   origin, dest [1]

  <chr>  <chr> <chr>                <dbl> <chr>                 <dbl>      <dbl>
1 JFK    ATL   Endeavor Air Inc.     1.40 ExpressJet Airli…       128       127.


Determine the reason for this difference


4.6.5 Answer:

airline_flight %>% 
  filter(origin == "JFK", dest == "ATL") %>% 
  group_by(carrier,name) %>% 
  summarise(num_flights = n(), avg_dep_delay = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE), mean_arr_delay = mean(arr_delay)) %>% 
  relocate(carrier, name)

1 9E      Endeavor Air Inc.                 55          1.47             NA
2 DL      Delta Air Lines Inc.            1874         10.7              NA
3 EV      ExpressJet Airlines Inc.           1        124               128


The EV carrier only has 1 flight that was delayed by 124 minutes, therefore the average delay will be higher than the other carriers that have had multiple flights.
